title: Reclaiming Homemaker as a Feminist Identity
subtitle: A reading on how domestic work became a pejorative and why that framing deserves scrutiny
excerpt: A reading on the history of how homemaking became devalued, the distinction between choosing domestic work and being confined to it, and what reclaiming the identity actually looks like in practice.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on feminist labour theory and domestic labour scholarship.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you have chosen domestic work as a significant part of your life and want a frame for holding that choice that does not require apologising for it.

content.text: Homemaker became a word that needed defending. It was not always so. Understanding how that happened makes it easier to decide whether you want to reclaim it.
How domestic work became a pejorative

After the war, paid work became the measure of contribution, and unpaid domestic work was quietly treated as not-work. Second-wave feminism was right to identify the home as a place of confinement for women. But in some quarters the argument slid from condemning the confinement to condemning the work. The problem was always who was required to do it; the work itself got caught in the crossfire.

Choosing domestic work is different from being confined to it. This distinction is the crux of the reclaim. The problem with the historical homemaker was not the work. It was the absence of choice, the lack of financial independence, and the cultural message that a woman without a domestic role had no value. Declaring that domestic work is worthless only reverses the last of those problems. It does not address the others. It simply adds a new version of the same trap.

Running a household well requires planning meals, managing a budget, keeping track of schedules, handling maintenance and buying decisions, and caring for the emotional needs of the people in the house. None of this is simple. Much of it is invisible precisely because it is done well.

Reclaiming is not performing enthusiasm or insisting the work is glamorous. It is naming the skills accurately, valuing the work on its own terms, and not prefacing a description of your days with an apology. It means not performing discomfort when someone asks what you do and the answer involves a lot of unpaid domestic work.

The feminist argument was never that domestic work is worthless. It was that women should not be the only ones doing it, and should not be trapped into doing it without choice. If you have the choice and you are making it, the apology is not required.
Original to homemade.education. Draws on feminist labour history.
